title: Confusion in a patient with cirrhosis

chart.oneLiner: 60M with known cirrhosis is brought in by family for 2 days of progressive confusion and excessive sleepiness.

opening: Where… where am I? What day is it?
patientFile:
PERSONA: 60-year-old man, drowsy, disoriented to time and place, slow to respond. A POOR historian — gives short, vague, sometimes incorrect answers.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student asks family/EMS/chart/medication list): Family reports 2 days of worsening confusion, day-night reversal, and sleeping much of the day. Known cirrhosis (alcohol-related). He has been constipated for several days and ran out of his lactulose about a week ago. No fall or head trauma. No fever noticed. Recently had dark stools once (possible GI bleed). MEDS: lactulose (out), spironolactone, propranolol.

HPI (from patient — limited): admits feeling 'foggy,' not sure how long. Denies headache, denies focal weakness.
PMH: cirrhosis, portal hypertension. SOCIAL: long history of heavy alcohol use, quit recently per family.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: drowsy, jaundiced (scleral icterus).
- Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 92, RR 16, 37.2°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Neuro: disoriented; ASTERIXIS present on wrist extension; no focal motor/sensory deficit; reflexes symmetric.
- Abdomen: distended with shifting dullness (ascites), nontender; caput/spider angiomata if examined.
- Skin: jaundice, palmar erythema. No signs of head trauma.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- The patient is confused: give brief, vague, disoriented answers. Provide COLLATERAL details only when the student explicitly asks family/EMS/nursing/chart or the medication list.
- Asterixis, ascites, and jaundice are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Glucose: Fingerstick glucose 96 — normal (always checked first in AMS).
labs.LFTs: AST/ALT mildly elevated, bilirubin elevated, albumin low, INR prolonged.
labs.Infection_workup: UA, blood cultures, and diagnostic paracentesis (to exclude SBP) — paracentesis: no neutrocytosis this time.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Tall peaked P in II (right atrial) plus broad notched P / deep terminal negativity in V1 (left atrial).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Tall peaked P in II (right atrial) plus broad notched P / deep terminal negativity in V1 (left atrial). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to screen for an infectious precipitant).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal PA chest film.
cxr.imageDescription: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to screen for an infectious precipitant).

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your assessment, list your differential for this patient's altered mental status (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Hepatic encephalopathy (leading,7). Hypoglycemia (CANT-MISS — always exclude,4). Intracranial hemorrhage (CANT-MISS in a coagulopathic cirrhotic,4). Infection/sepsis incl. SBP (CANT-MISS,4). Electrolyte/metabolic derangement (core,3). Drug/toxin (core,2). Named leading dx w/ justification — cirrhosis, asterixis, missed lactulose/constipation (core,1).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Hepatic encephalopathy. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Hypoglycemia; Intracranial hemorrhage; SBP. Also reasonable: Electrolyte/metabolic derangement; Drug/toxin.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — cirrhosis, asterixis, missed lactulose/constipation

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Fingerstick GLUCOSE first (CANT-MISS,3). BMP/electrolytes (2). Ammonia (2). LFTs/coags (2). CBC (2). Infection workup — UA, cultures, CXR, diagnostic paracentesis for SBP (3). CT head if focal deficit/trauma/anticoagulated (1). PENALTY: anchoring without excluding hypoglycemia or infection (-2).

penalties.item: anchoring without excluding hypoglycemia or infection

contraindicatedTests: anchoring without excluding hypoglycemia or infection
orderSensitiveActions: anchoring without excluding hypoglycemia or infection

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = normal sinus rhythm, no acute changes. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes a normal tracing (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-reading (-2).
keyFindings: left atrial enlargement (broad notched P / V1 terminal force)
steps.idealAnswer: Biatrial enlargement: peaked P waves in II with a broad biphasic P in V1.

coreActions: left atrial enlargement (broad notched P / V1 terminal force)

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no consolidation/effusion. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); explains it screens for an infectious precipitant (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: explains it screens for an infectious precipitant
criticalFindings: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to screen for an infectious precipitant).
steps.idealAnswer: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to screen for an infectious precipitant).

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to screen for an infectious precipitant).

coreActions.item: explains it screens for an infectious precipitant

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the elevated ammonia, normal glucose, and the history, what is your diagnosis and the likely precipitant?
steps.rubric: Hepatic encephalopathy (5). Names a precipitant — constipation / missed lactulose / possible GI bleed (3). Notes ammonia supports but does not alone diagnose; it is clinical (bonus,2).

bonusActions.item: Notes ammonia supports but does not alone diagnose; it is clinical

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Identify and treat the precipitant (CANT-MISS,6). Lactulose titrated to 2–3 soft stools/day (CANT-MISS,7). Add rifaximin (4). Treat any infection/GI bleed found (4). Avoid sedatives/benzodiazepines (2). Supportive care, monitor airway/mental status (2). PENALTY: sedating the patient (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Plan: Identify and treat the precipitant; Lactulose titrated to 2–3 soft stools/day.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 60M with known cirrhosis is brought in by family for 2 days of progressive confusion and excessive sleepiness. The diagnosis is Hepatic encephalopathy, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: distended with shifting dullness (ascites), nontender; caput/spider angiomata if examined.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Hepatic encephalopathy while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Hepatic encephalopathy and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalInterpretationItems: Normal sinus rhythm; no acute changes.
No consolidation or effusion (obtained to screen for an infectious precipitant).
keyImageInterpretationFindings: No consolidation or effusion (obtained to screen for an infectious precipitant).

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
